coredata.dc:title: Magnetic properties of Al3+ doped Fe3O4 by solid-solid reaction and biological applications of GQDs grafted MNPs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Huan, Weiwei
Dong, Mengyang
Li, Jie
Xie, Meng
Huang, Yan
Carlini, Riccardo
Yang, Yuxiang
coredata.dc:description: 2.79 % Al3+ doped Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Al-Fe3O4 MNPs) was prepared by a solid–solid reaction between aluminum formic acid hydrazide and as-prepared Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles. The obtained Al-Fe3O4 MNPs were strongly ferromagnetic, and their greatest saturation magnetization value was 82.3 electromagnetic units per gram (emu/g). The Al-Fe3O4 MNPs were subsequently coated with SiO2. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Mössbauer spectroscopy, and vibrating-sample magnetometry (VSM) were used to characterize the morphology, structure, magnetic properties, and doping mechanism of the doped magnetic particles, respectively. Then graphene quantum dot (GQD) is loaded on the surface of aminated Al-Fe3O4@SiO2 (AFS) MNPs by a covalent connection, and Al-Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs (AFSG) MNPs. Furthermore, AFSG MNPs were labeled with the metastable nuclear isomer of technetium-99 (99mTc) and their in vivo targeting performance was investigated. The AFSG-99mTc MNPs demonstrated a relatively strong magnetic response in a pulsed magnetic gradient field. The data obtained from the control and experimental groups indicated that the ability to uptake radioactivity of the livers of New Zealand rabbits via the magnetic targeted intervention was 3.69 times higher than that without the magnetic targeted intervention.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3O4 nanoparticle
Al3+-doped
Solid-solid reaction
Magnetic property
GQDs grafted

Targeting capability of AFSG-99mTc MNPs in vivo
Tumor targeting experiments and single photoemission-computed tomography (SPECT) imaging
Changes to the saturation magnetization of Fe3O4 with the doping amount
XPS characterization of the AFSG-99mTc MNPs
Targeting capabilities of AFSG-99mTc MNPs in vivo

The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 20577010 and 20971043), and by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Energy dispersive spectroscopy spectra of Fe3O4 MNPs with various actual Al3+ doping amounts: a) 0.93%; b) 1.43%; c) 2.79%; d) 2.99%; e) 4.57%; f) 8.30% *The corresponding theoretical Al3+ doping contents were 2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, and 30%, respectively.
TEM images of Fe3O4 particles with various Al3+ doping amounts: a) pure Al; b) 0.93 %; c) 2.79 %; d) 2.99 %; e) Al(2.79 %) Fe3O4@SiO2; f) HRSEM image of AFS MNPs.

(a) Laser particle size analyzer results for AFS MNPs and (b) XRD pattern of the nano-Fe3O4 particles. (c) XRD patterns of the Fe3O4 particles with various theoretical Al3+ contents. (d) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs and AFSG MNPs.
Changes of δ (a) and ε (b) with Al doping amount at different 2θ values according to location of the peaks.
(a) Mössbauer spectrum of pure Fe3O4 MNPs. Mössbauer spectra of Fe3O4 MNPs with two different degrees of Al3+ doping: b) Al(0.93 %)-Fe3O4; c) Al(2.79 %)-Fe3O4.
(a) Saturation magnetization (Ms) curves of Fe3O4 particles with different Al3+ doped amounts. (b) ZFC-FC curves of AFSG MNPs.
XPS of AFS-NH2 MNPs and AFSG MNPs.
Cell viability of VX2 cells after incubation for 24 or 48 h with various concentrations of AFSG MNPs.

The γ data representing %ID/g in a), b), c), and d) were obtained after 15, 60, 120, and 180 min, respectively (%ID/g = percent injected dose per gram of tissue; mice 1 = without magnetic targeting; mice 2 = with magnetic targeting).
SPECT images of New Zealand rabbits after injection of AFSG MNPs. a) without targeting, b) with targeting by a pulsed magnetic field.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of AFSG MNPs and the corresponding biological target application.
Reaction mechanism for the formation of Al-Fe3O4 *aluminum formate containing hydrazine.
CHN elemental chemical analysis of aluminum formate containing hydrazine.
Results for Rietveld analysis of powder X-ray diffraction pattern of Al-Fe3O4 MNPs cubic symmetry).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: D (nm) is the calculated value; Do (nm) is the original value.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mössbauer parameters from the spectra produced by Fe3O4 MNPs and Al-Fe3O4 MNPs obtained via a solid–solid reaction.
Magnetic parameters of Fe3O4 particles with different Al3+ doping amounts.

head.ce:title: Magnetic properties of Al3+ doped Fe3O4 by solid-solid reaction and biological applications of GQDs grafted MNPs

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the present work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2.79 % Al3+ doped Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Al-Fe3O4 MNPs) was prepared by a solid–solid reaction between aluminum formic acid hydrazide and as-prepared Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles. The obtained Al-Fe3O4 MNPs were strongly ferromagnetic, and their greatest saturation magnetization value was 82.3 electromagnetic units per gram (emu/g). The Al-Fe3O4 MNPs were subsequently coated with SiO2. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Mössbauer spectroscopy, and vibrating-sample magnetometry (VSM) were used to characterize the morphology, structure, magnetic properties, and doping mechanism of the doped magnetic particles, respectively. Then graphene quantum dot (GQD) is loaded on the surface of aminated Al-Fe3O4@SiO2 (AFS) MNPs by a covalent connection, and Al-Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs (AFSG) MNPs. Furthermore, AFSG MNPs were labeled with the metastable nuclear isomer of technetium-99 (99mTc) and their in vivo targeting performance was investigated. The AFSG-99mTc MNPs demonstrated a relatively strong magnetic response in a pulsed magnetic gradient field. The data obtained from the control and experimental groups indicated that the ability to uptake radioactivity of the livers of New Zealand rabbits via the magnetic targeted intervention was 3.69 times higher than that without the magnetic targeted intervention.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Although polymer-based nanomaterials are used for targeted drug delivery systems, they are incapable of enabling continuous observation through imaging owing to their poor contrast capability. Nanocarriers with magnetic characteristics have an advantage over conventional nonmagnetic carriers because the targeting mechanism [1] can be controlled very precisely by guiding the magnetic carriers through an externally applied magnetic field [2–5]. Maghemite (Fe3O4) is the most commonly used magnetic targeting [6–12] material in cancer therapy because it is a sensitive contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and produces superior contrast [13–15]. Recent findings indicate that Fe3O4 MNPs produce a weak magnetic signal within biological systems, posing a critical challenge for cancer treatment [16–19]. Therefore, a method that improves the magnetic properties of such nanoparticles is needed to meet the demands of cancer therapy [20].

As reported [21–23], contrast enhancement in MRI is achieved by changing the magnetic relaxation times T1 (positive contrast agents) and T2 (negative contrast agents) of the hydrogen protons contained in the tissue. Iron oxide MNPs exhibit an especially short T2, and are called T2-contrast agents. A short T2 is equivalent to high R2 relaxivity. The shorter the T2, the higher the obtained T2 weighted contrast. Increasing saturation magnetization enhances the R2 relaxivity of MNPs, leading to improved contrast in MRI, as demonstrated by studies conducted by Lee and Hyeon [24]. A large saturation magnetization (Ms) and a superparamagnetic nature are the basic features of magnetic composite materials, especially Fe3O4 MNPs. A pure Fe3O4 phase has an inverse spinel structure described by the formula Fe2+(Fe3+)2O4, where Fe3+ is split between tetrahedral sites (A-sites) and octahedral sites, and Fe2+ occupies only the octahedral sites (B-sites). Recently, numerous authors have attempted to improve the magnetic properties of Fe3O4 by doping it with magnetic metal ions with large Ms values [25–29]. If the A-site ions are doped by cations with a lower magnetic moment than that of Fe3+, or the B-site ions are doped by cations with a higher magnetic moment than that of Fe3+, the net saturated magnetic moment of the spinel ferrite Fe3O4 increases [30–33]. Many efforts have already been made to increase Ms by doping with strongly magnetic metal ions, such as Co2+ or Dy3+, at the B-sites via sol–gel [25] or wet chemical [26] methods, or by co-precipitation followed by thermal reduction [27]. The Ms values of γ-Fe2O3 and MxFe3-xO4 NPs can be increased to 76.65 emu/g. The doping of Mn2+ and Ni2+ at B-sites has also been studied by Deepak et al [28]. and Tago et al [29]. However, these studies involved the use of toxic heavy metal [34–38] ions, which are highly undesirable for cancer treatment. Therefore, a harmless method for improving the magnetic properties of maghemite is urgently needed. Al3+ and Zn2+ ions are excellent alternatives to heavy metal ions owing to their low toxicity. For example, Liu et al. used non-magnetic Zn2+ ions to dope the A-sites of Fe3O4 MNPs to prepare Zn x Fe3-
x O4 by co-precipitation [39]. However, there has been no investigation of the main group metal Al3+ in this context to date.

In the present work, we incorporated Al3+ into Fe3O4 NPs. The Fe3O4 NPs were produced by co-precipitation, and immediately ground with non-magnetic Al3+ formate containing hydrazine at a molar doping ratio of 2 %-10 % at room temperature. The mixture was then calcined at 350 °C to prepare Al3+ doped Fe3O4 NPs. The influence of the amount of doping Al3+ on saturation magnetization and coercivity was also studied. Subsequently, the Al-Fe3O4 MNPs with the highest saturation magnetization (82.30 emu/g) produced using an Al-doping content of 2.79 % were coated with a SiO2, then grafted with GQDs [40] for in vivo targeting studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 and Al-Fe3O4 MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Fe3O4 MNPs was consistent with the previous report [41], and the aluminum (or magnesium) formate containing hydrazine was prepared by adding an 85 % hydrazine hydrate solution dropwise to a formic acid solution of anhydrous aluminum trichloride (or magnesium chloride), and precipitating the product in anhydrous ethanol. Further details of the method are described in the Text. S1. The carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen contents of the aluminum formate containing hydrazine were quantitatively determined by CHN elemental chemical analysis. The content of Al3+ doped Fe3O4 MNPs was determined using a TJA IRIS1000 inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) system.

(3) The Al3+ doped Fe3O4 MNPs was synthesized at room temperature by solid–solid grinding nano Fe3O4 with 2 %, 4 %, 6 %, 8 %, 10 %, or 30 % aluminum formate containing hydrazine while maintaining a constant total molar quantity of ferric ions and doping ions. The nitrogen was protected during grinding by continuously flushing with nitrogen gas. Subsequently, the as-synthesized doped Fe3O4 MNPs were calcined in an electrical resistance furnace tube 350 °C for 3 h under the N2 atmosphere.

First, the bipolar surfactant tetramethylammonium 11-aminoundecanoate was prepared according to the method reported by Ankamwar et al [42]. The hydrophobic Al-Fe3O4 MNPs were then treated with the bipolar surfactant to produce hydrophilic MNPs.

The Al-Fe3O4 particles were dispersed in hexane containing 75 mM oleic acid and 75 mM oleylamine to produce a hydrophobic Fe3O4 colloid. To convert the hydrophobic Fe3O4 colloid into hydrophilic doped Fe3O4 MNPs, 0.07 g of the bipolar surfactant was suspended in 5.0 mL of dichloromethane, and 0.10 mL of the hydrophobic Fe3O4 colloid was added. The mixture was placed on a shaker for approximately 1.5 h. This completely transformed the polarity of the doped Fe3O4 particles. The hydrophilic doped Fe3O4 MNPs were then separated at 7000 rpm for 15 min.

Subsequently, 50 mg of the hydrophilic doped Fe3O4 MNPs was ultrasonically dispersed in 100 mL of anhydrous alcohol, and 6 mL of NH3 and 0.30 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were slowly dropped in turn at regular intervals while stirring vigorously. After stirring continuously for 8 h at 25 °C, the resulting mixture was centrifuged to wash away uncoated silica leaving a AFS MNPs sample.

To prepare amino-modified samples, dried AFS MNPs samples were re-suspended in dry toluene (50 mL), and 1 mL of 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) was added. The mixture was then heated to reflux for 12 h. The amino-modified magnetic nanocomposites were separated and washed with ethanol and water, then dried for 12 h.

To prepare the GQDs-grafted AFS MNPs, 50 mg of each amino-modified magnetic nanocomposite was dispersed in 37 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide, and the resulting mixture was treated with ultrasound for 30 min. The MAH-GQDs (20 mg) were then dropped into the mixed solution, and the procedures for preparing MAH-GQDs are outlined in the Text. S2). MAH-GQDs (20 mg) After allowing the mixed solution to react for 12 h at room temperature while stirring magnetically, the AFSG MNPs product was magnetically separated, washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water (three times with each), and placed in a vacuum drying oven for 12 h. AFSG MNPs samples were The obtained, and the Schematic illustration was listed in Scheme 1 .

The morphology and size of the samples were determined using a JEM-2010F field emission transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV. The magnetic investigations were performed using a Quantum Design MPMS-7 magnetometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al (Mono) X-ray excitation source.

Mössbauer spectra of the 57Fe nuclei in each sample were recorded using an OXFORD MS-500 model constant acceleration Mössbauer spectrometer with a 1024 multichannel analyzer at 298 K. The velocity was calibrated with an α-Fe foil. The radiation source was 57Co/Rh. A xenon (methane) proportional counter was used as a detector.

The cytotoxicity of the material was investigated using a rabbit anaplastic epidermis squamous cell carcinoma (VX2) cell line. The VX2 cells were cultured at 37 °C in Eagle’s minimum essential cell culture medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % double antibody in an incubator containing 5 % CO2. A 96-well plate was inoculated with the cells, which were cultured for 24 h at a certain density, then various concentrations of materials (0, 25, 50, 100, 150, or 200 μg/mL) were co-cultured with the cells for a further 24 h or 48 h. The survival rate of the cells was measured by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. After incubation, the culture solution was aspirated, the cells were washed once with phosphate-buffered saline, and an appropriate amount of MTT solution was added to each well. After culturing for 4 h, the supernatant of each well was removed, and dimethyl sulfoxide solution was added to dissolve the crystals. Finally, the absorbance at 570 nm was measured using an enzyme-plate analyzer (DNM-9602). Cells cultured in pure medium were taken as a blank control. The cell survival rate was calculated according to the following formula:

Cell Viability (%) =.
OD
sample
-
OD
blank
OD
control
-
OD
blank
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting capability of AFSG-99mTc MNPs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: 50 mg/mL SnCl2 with 1 mol/L HCl solution and 1 mg/mL vitamin C (ascorbic acid) aqueous solution were prepared. An SnCl2 solution was then obtained using 5 μL of 50 mg/mL SnCl2 diluted with 5 mL of 1 mg/mL ascorbic acid solution. Subsequently, 20 mg of 2.79 % AFSG MNPs was dispersed in schering bottles that had been pre-filled with 2 mL of medical physiological saline. Next, 400 μL of the SnCl2 diluent and 100 μL of Na99mTcO4 with a radiation intensity of approximately 5 mCi (mCi) were added insequence. The mixture was shaken uniformly, and kept at room temperature for 30 min. Subsequently, the obtained product, which was labelled AFSG MNPs, was washed with physiological saline, and exposed three times to a strong magnet to remove excess free 99mTc. The purified AFSG-99mTc MNPs was re-dispersed in physiological saline solution and subjected to supersonic treatment to generate 20 mg/2.5 mL of labeled compound solution.

To investigate the in vivo distribution of the magnetic nanoparticles in a variety of tissues, female ICR mice were selected as an animal model to determine the relative in vivo concentration of AFSG-99mTc MNPs in tissues.

Healthy female mice were injected with AFSG-99mTc MNPs fluid via their tail veins (0.1 mL of 0.1 mCi fluid per mouse at a dose of 10 mg). The mice were then sacrificed at 15, 60, 120 and 180 min intervals after injection. Blood, heart, lung, kidney, liver, spleen, pancreas, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, brain, skeletal muscle, and bone tissues were collected to determine the radioactivity counts. The extent of 99mTc labeling in each of these thirteen collected organ tissue samples was determined using a gamma counter. The liver and spleen tissues from the mice were exposed to a targeting gradient pulsed magnetic field of a certain magnetic field strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tumor targeting experiments and single photoemission-computed tomography (SPECT) imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Two healthy male New Zealand rabbits were intravenously injected with 0.5 mL of 20 mg/2.5 mL purified AFSG-99mTc MNPs solution via their marginal ear veins. To prevent blood clotting, 1 mL of 3 % pentobarbital, 0.5 mL of heparin sodium, and 0.5 mL of medical physiological saline were sequentially injected. After 1–2 h, the natural distribution, SPECT static images, and half quantitative analysis calculations were obtained during SPECT imaging. The natural distribution of the AFSG-99mTc in the kidney, liver, lung, and bladder tissues of the New Zealand rabbits were obtained to determine the various 99mTc radioactive count ratios of the tissues.

The livers of the New Zealand rabbits were then placed on the magnetic pole of a YMC-11 medical pulsed magnetic field generator. The output voltages of the pulsed magnetic fields were set at 500 V for 2 h, the frequency was set at 1.5 Hz, and the gradient magnetic field strength of the targeted location was 683 mT. This was done to demonstrate the magnetic targeting effects of the AFSG-99mTc MNPs. Static SPECT imaging was performed to determine the uptake of AFSG-99mTc MNPs by the kidney, liver, lung, and bladder tissues.

The Al3+ doped Fe3O4 MNPs were characterized by energy dispersive spectroscopy. The results indicated that when the theoretical Al3+ doping contents in Fe3O4 MNPs were 2 %, 4 %, 6 %, 8 %, 10 %, or 30 %, the actual Al3+ doping amounts were 0.90 %, 1.41 %, 2.79 %, 2.96 %, 4.55 %, or 8.26 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1 .

Fig. 2 shows TEM images of Fe3O4 MNPs with various Al3+ doping ratios. As shown in Fig. 2a, the Fe3O4 MNPs were of uniform particle size (approximately 20 nm) and were well dispersed. The morphology and size of the Fe3O4 MNPs changed to some extent according to the Al3+ doping amount. When the Al3+ doping amount fell within the range 0.93 % to 2.79 %, the Fe3O4 MNPs were uniformly dispersed and were 17–20 nm in size, as shown in Fig. 2b and c. However, when the Al3+ doping amount increased beyond 2.99 %, the Fe3O4 MNPs are not well dispersed and their measured size was 25–29 nm, as shown in Fig. 2d. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2e indicates that AFS MNPs (2.79 % doped) had a well-dispersed core–shell structure. Base on this, the following section select the AFS MNPs as the ideal model. The average diameter of the particles was approximately 55 nm with an iron core of 35 nm and a SiO2 of 5 nm. The high-magnification image shown in the inset of Fig. 2e reveals the obvious core–shell structure of AFS MNPs, which comprised a self-assembled core of Al-Fe3O4 inside a SiO2 shell. The size and morphology of AFS MNPs was further studied by HRSEM. The HRSEM image in Fig. 2f reveals the spherical shape of AFS MNPs with a particle size of approximately 58 nm; it agrees well with the results shown in Fig. 2e.
The results shown in Table 1 show that the precursor formula of the aluminum formate containing hydrazine was [Al(HCOO)3]0.5·[N2H5Cl]1.5·HCOON2H5.
The reaction mechanism for the formation of Al(dop%)-Fe3O4 using aluminum formate containing hydrazine and the newly-formed Fe3O4 MNPs is represented in Scheme 2 .

A laser scattering particle analyzer was also used to determine the average particle size. Fig. 3 a reveals that the effective dynamic diameter of AFS MNPs was 95.97 ± 7.67 nm. Fig. 3b shows the XRD patterns of the Fe3O4 MNPs. The XRD patterns agree well with the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) standard diffraction card number 79–0418 for Fe3O4 with a face-centered cubic inverse spinel structure. The typical peaks at 18°, 30.1°, 35.5°, 37.1°, 43.1°, 53.5°, 57.0°, 62.67°, 71.00°, 74.13°, and 79.21° reported in Fig. 3b correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), (533), and (444) crystal planes [41,43], respectively (the numbers in parentheses represent the Miller indices).

The XRD patterns of the Fe3O4 MNPs with various theoretical Al3+ doping contents are shown in Fig. 3c. It is obvious from the figure that all the doped Fe3O4 MNPs produced essentially the same diffraction patterns as that of the pure Fe3O4 MNPs, independent of the content and species of the doping nonmagnetic metal ions. No apparent XRD patterns attributable to other phases were detected. Furthermore, there was little difference between the intensities of the detectable diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of the pure Fe3O4 MNPs and those of the doped Fe3O4 MNPs. This result demonstrates that the doped nonmagnetic metal ions were uniformly distributed on the Fe3O4 lattice and that the product essentially comprised a uniformly distributed phase and not a mixture of phases. Moreover, the doping process was incapable of forming a new phase, and the face-centered cubic inverse spinel structure of the Al-Fe3O4 MNPs phase was retained. The results shown in Table 2 for indexing the powder X-ray diffraction pattern of Fig. 3c demonstrate these properties. This was due to the replacement of larger Fe3+ (0.067 nm) ions with smaller Al3+ ions (0.051 nm) at the tetrahedral sites (A-sites), leading to the formation of a new phase of hercynite, and causing a smaller increase in the lattice parameter.

In order to better explain the effects of doping amount on the Al3+-doped Fe3O4, the dislocation density (δ (nm−2)) and micro-strain (ε) were calculated with the help of W-H plot. The OriginLab was used to find the parameters of 2θ and FWHM value (β1/2) from XRD data, the crystallites size D (nm) was calculated through Scherer equation
D =
0.9 λ
β
1 / 2
cos θ
=
0.9 × 0.1542 × 57.3
β
1 / 2
cos θ
. δ was calculated by the equation
δ =
1
D 2
, and ε calculated by
ε =
β
1 / 2
4 tan θ × 57.3
. The results were shown in Table S2a–e.

Plot of δ and ε to Al doping amount gave the changes of δ and ε with Al doping amount at different 2θ values according to location of the peaks, as can be seen in Fig. 4 a. From Fig. 4b, we can find the values of δ and ε increase with Al doping amount at different 2θ values, when the Al doping amount gets at 2.79 %, the values of δ and ε attain maximum value. Due to the fact that dislocation is a defect in the crystal, high dislocation density will lead to more defects in the crystal, thereby reducing the integrity and order of the crystal, so that the crystallinity is reduced significantly, and Al-Fe3O4(2.79 %) has the lowest crystallinity among all Al3+ doped Fe3O4. 2.79 % doping amount of aluminum causes lattice distortion of Fe3O4. But the values of δ and ε decrease with Al doping amount after 2.79 % doping amount of aluminum. This is because the more Al doping amount can stabilize the deformed Fe3O4 lattice instead, and promote the formation of new solid solution [44].

Fig. 3d shows the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs and AFSG MNPs. The GQDs produced strong fluorescence at 421 nm when excited by radiation of 345 nm wavelength. After grafting with AFS-NH2 MNPs, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs was quenched to a certain extent owing to the introduction of AFS-NH2 MNPs. This quenched the fluorescence of the GQDs, in keeping with the literature reports, indicating the successful grafting of AFS-NH2 MNPs with MAH-GQDs.

A Mössbauer spectrum can reveal comparatively small variations in the magnetic structure and coordination structure around the Mössbauer atom in the nonmagnetic metal ions of a solid–solid-doped Fe3O4 lattice. Mössbauer spectra and parameters are rather sensitive to any variation in the doping amount in the chemical environment around the Mössbauer atom.

Fig. 5 shows the Mössbauer spectra of pure Fe3O4 and Al-Fe3O4 MNPs obtained at room temperature. Both spectra comprise two distinct sextets, indicating the ferromagnetic properties of the samples. The Fe3O4 lattice comprises an inversespinel structure with A-sites and B-sites, and the two sextets correspond to the A and B sites of the spinel structure [45]. In the two sextets, the broader sextet lines with relatively larger isomer shifts (δ) represent Fe3+ and Fe2+ at the B-sites in the lattice of Fe3O4, whereas the narrower sextet lines with relatively smaller δ represent Fe3+ at the A-sites in the lattice of Fe3O4. The central doublet demonstrates that the pure Fe3O4 and Al-Fe3O4 MNPs exhibited apparent superpara magnetism.

The coexistence of the sextet and the superparamagnetic doublet in the Mössbauer spectra of pure Fe3O4, Al(0.93 %)-Fe3O4, and Al(2.79 %)-Fe3O4 can probably be attributed to their average particle diameters, which have been measured respectively. According to RJ Joseyphus [45], the super-para magnetic limit occurs at a particle size of approximately 25 nm.

Although the experimental Mössbauer spectrum of Al (0.93 %)-Fe3O4 comprises two distinct sextets with a central doublet, it differs significantly from the spectra of both pure Fe3O4 and Al(2.79 %)-Fe3O4. The distance between the two peaks of the central doublet in the inner sextets is noticeably less than the corresponding distances in the spectra of both pure Fe3O4 and Al(2.79 %)-Fe3O4, probably because the lattice parameter of Al(0.93 %)-Fe3O4 is only 0.8381 nm owing to its small particle size. This experimental result demonstrates the transformation of the magnetic structure of a sample with the doping amount of Al3+.

The fitted Mössbauer parameters obtained from the experimental spectra produced by the samples are given in Table 3 . As the doping amount of Al3+ increased, the δ values of Fe3+ and Fe2+ at the A- and B-sites changed little. The hyperfine magnetic field (Hhf) value increased on the whole, whereas the area ratios of Fe3+and Fe2+ at the A- and B-sites changed accordingly. In particular, the area ratio of Fe3+ at the A-sites decreased significantly from 33.6 % to 26.9 %. As shown in Table 3, the δ value represents the isomer shift, indicating the extranuclear electron configuration. QS corresponds to the electrical quadrupole splitting of a nucleus, which interacts with the electric field gradient around the nucleus and generates the splitting of the nuclear energy level. The Hhf is also known as the internal magnetic field, and is determined by the magnetic moment generated by the incompletely filled electron shell of a magnetic ion. Γ(mm/s) represents the width of the peak at half height, and the ratios represent the contents of Fe3+ or Fe2+, which are assigned to the ratios of iron of a particular chemical valence at the various sites to the total iron content.

According to the literature [46,47], a large hyperfine field with a small isomer shift is characteristic of Fe3+ ions at a tetrahedral A-site, whereas the other two sextets with relatively large isomer shifts represent Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions at two different B-site environments. The A-site isomer shift is expected to be smaller than the B-site isomer shift owing to a higher degree of covalency at the A-site [45]. Therefore, the Mössbauer spectrum data for the Fe3+ ions can be divided into two groups, according to the following rule: the group with the larger isomer shift of Fe3+ ions is assigned to B-site Fe3+, whereas the group with the smaller isomer shift of Fe3+ ions is assigned to A-site Fe3+. According to R.N. Panda [48], the isomer shifts 0.64 ± 0.02, 0.66 ± 0.01, and 0.69 ± 0 mm/s listed in Table 3 fall into the range of values expected for Fe2+ ions at B-sites with octahedral coordination. Therefore, the ratio of Fe3+ to Fe2+ for pure Fe3O4 can be calculated according to the area ratio of Fe3+ and Fe2+ at A- and B-sites, and according to the results, the ratio of Fe3+ to Fe2+ is close to 2:1, which agrees well with the standard ratio of Fe3+ to Fe2+ in a Fe3O4 lattice.

As shown in Table 3, when the doping amount of Al3+ in the Fe3O4 MNPs increased from 0 % to 0.93 % or 2.79 %, the Fe3+ content at the A-sites decreased from 33.6 % to 28.0 % and 26.9 %, respectively. These findings show that Al3+ ions have a strong preference for A-sites. Consequently, the Fe3+ ions at the A-sites are replaced by Al3+ ions, and hence the content of Fe3+ ions at the A-sites decreases accordingly. This occurs because the ionic radius of an Al3+ ion (0.051 nm) is less than that of the replaced Fe3+ ion (0.064 nm), and rAl 3+(0.051 nm)/rO 2-(0.132 nm) = 0.39. Therefore, the smaller Al3 + ion is very likely to replace the slightly larger Fe3+ ion, and tends to occupy tetrahedral A-sites in a perfect doping scenario. However, when some of the Fe3+ ions at the A-sites are replaced by Al3+ ions with smaller radii, the original Fe3+ ions at the A-sites are forced to migrate from the A- to the B-sites, leading to an increase in Fe3+ ion content at the B-sites. As shown in Table 3, the area ratio of Fe3+ at the B-sites increased to 38.8 % when the doping amount of Al3+ ions was 0.93 %, but the area ratio of Fe3+ at the B-sites decreased to 33.4 % when the doping amount of Al3+ ions increased to 2.79 %, probably because some of the Fe3+ migrated to the surfaces of the Fe3O4 MNPs. Otherwise, the area ratio of Fe2+ at the B-sites increased from 34.3 % to 39.7 % when the doping amount of Al3+ increased to 2.79 %, demonstrating that the occupying Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions at the B-sites were significantly affected by doping with Al3+ ions at the A-sites [49].

It is also worth noting that the QS parameter of Fe3+ at the A-sites decreased significantly after doping with Al3+ ions because Al3+ ions with smaller ionic radii replaced Fe3+ ions at the A-sites, leading to a smaller quadrupole splitting and an increase in symmetry around the A-sites. However, the QS parameter of Fe3+ at the B-sites changed little, indicating that there was little change in the quadrupole splitting of Fe3+ at the B-sites. The changes in the QS parameter of Fe2+ at the B-sites differed from those in the two scenarios described above; when the doping amount of Al3+ ions was 0.93 %, the QS parameter decreased from 0.19 to 0.02, indicating an increase in symmetry around the Fe2+ at the B-sites. However, when the doping amount of Al3+ ions was 2.79 %, the QS parameter decreased to −0.52, possibly because a large doping amount of Al3+ ions leads to distortion or random strain around the Fe2+ at the B-sites, which lowers the symmetry. These findings were demonstrated when the lattice parameter of the 2.79 % Al-Fe3O4 MNPs particles reached a maximum value of 0.8423 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes to the saturation magnetization of Fe3O4 with the doping amount
ce:section.ce:para: All the magnetic parameters, i.e., Ms, remanence (Mr), coercivity (Hc), and the Mr/Ms ratio, are listed in Table 4 , and compared with the reported literature (Fig. S1). Magnetization hysteresis curves of Fe3O4 particles with different Al3+ doping amounts are shown in Fig. 5a.

Fig. 6 a shows the Ms of Fe3O4 MNPs changed with various doping amounts of Al3+. When the Al3+ doping amount reached 0.93 %, the Ms had a first peak value of 76.59 emu/g. This resulted from the decrease in the amount of Fe3+ ions at the A-sites based on the Mössbauer spectrum analysis described above. Therefore, the total magnetic moment of the A-site ions certainly decreased. In view of the facts that the net molecular magnetic moment can be estimated from the difference between the total magnetic moment of the B-site ions and the total magnetic moment of the A-site ions, we can conclude that the net molecular magnetic moment of the doped Fe3O4 MNPs increased accordingly.

When the doping amount of Al3+ reached 2.79 %, the Ms had a second peak value of 82.30 emu/g, i.e., the maximum value of the doped Fe3O4 MNPs. This was owing to the fact that the Fe3+ ion content at the A-sites decreased significantly from 33.6 % to 26.9 %, and the Fe2+ ion content at the B-sites increased from 34.3 % to 39.7 %, making the increase in the net molecular magnetic moment inevitable. Al-Fe3O4 has a larger saturation magnetization value (82.3 emu/g) than that of Co-doped Fe3O4 (50.85 emu/g) [42]. Two different doping mechanisms are involved. The magnetic properties of Co-doped Fe3O4 chiefly depend on the distribution of Co2+ions in the Fe3O4 lattice, which favors the alignment of Co2+ spins parallel to the cube edge of the spinel lattice, leading to a small increase in saturation magnetization. In contrast, in Al-Fe3O4 MNPs, the A-site ions in the Fe3O4 lattice are replaced by Al3+ cations with a lower magnetic moment than Fe3+, because Al3+ prefers a tetrahedral environment. An increase in the absolute value of the difference between the magnetic moments at the A and B-sites leads to a significant increase in saturation magnetization.

The magnetization measurements were carried out using a commercial SQUID magnetometer in the temperature range 50–300 K and in applied magnetic fields of up to 199.913 Oe (Oe). The separation between the ZFC and FC curves took place at a lower temperature of 179.5 K owing to the smaller particle sizes [46]. The Fig. 6b also indicate the typical blocking process of an assembly of superparamagnetic particles with a distribution of blocking temperatures (see Fig. 6b at TB ≈ 145.5 K), where the TB is the super magnetic transition temperature of AFSG MNPs, at which the super magnetic particles change into ferromagnetic particles, forming numbers of irregular domains.

Table 4 shows the coercive force Hc of two doped Fe3O4 MNPs maintained at approximately 150 Oe, i.e., close to pure nano Fe3O4, when the actual doping amount of Al3+ was below 1.43 %, suggesting that Al-Fe3O4 MNPs are characterized by soft magnetic properties. These findings are possibly owing to the unchanged crystal lattice structure. When the doping amount of Al3+ was higher than 2.79 %, the coercive force Hc decreased to below 110 Oe, possibly owing to distortion, which resulted in a lowering of symmetry and an increase in crystalline magnetic anisotropy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS characterization of the AFSG-99mTc MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: XPS analyses were carried out to quantitatively determine the contents of carbon, aluminum, and silica. The resulting integrated spectra are shown in Fig. 7 a and b. There were two binding energy peaks attributable to 2p, i.e., Al2p and Si2p, at 74.2 and 102.9 eV, respectively, and one binding energy peak attributable to 1 s in C1s at 284.8 eV, indicating the presence of Al, Si, and C elements in both AFS-NH2 and AFSG MNPs. The relative concentrations of the elements were calculated as follows. In AFS-NH2, the atomic concentrations of carbon, aluminum, and silica were 65.65 %, 3.34 %, and 31.01 %, respectively, whereas in AFSG MNPs, the corresponding atomic concentrations were 67.46 %, 2.76 %, and 29.77 %, respectively. The obvious increase in the carbon content indicates that the GQDs had been grafted onto the AFS-NH2.

Fig. 8 indicates that after incubating various concentrations of the materials for 24 or 48 h, the survival fraction of the VX2 cells increased to 96.9 % after 24 h and 96.6 % after 48 h, indicating that AFSG MNPs had little cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting capabilities of AFSG-99mTc MNPs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 reveals that the uptake of AFSG-99mTc MNPs was significantly higher in liver tissues placed on the magnetic pole of a YMC-11 medical pulsed magnetic field generator than it was in non-targeted liver tissues. This demonstrates that the magnetic pole of a YMC-11 medical pulsed magnetic field generator facilitates the uptake of nano-magnetic particles from the blood by the targeted liver. It also suggests that the NPs have a significantly enhanced dual liver-targeting effect. The experimental results described above suggest that if the AFSG-99mTc MNPs are able to magnetically target a tumor location through the blood circulation, drug-loaded MNPs could certainly be induced to accumulate at the tumor [10]. This would provide the foundation for magnetic targeted drug therapy.

Fig. 10 a shows the results of the control group, i.e., the in vivo natural distribution of AFSG-99mTc MNPs in New Zealand rabbits without in vitro magnetic targeting. SPECT images obtained by whole-body TCT showed that the AFSG-99mTc MNPs were uniformly distributed throughout the liver. The level of uptake of radioactivity by the liver was determined using region-of-interest technology, and the liver was investigated by CT and normalized mutual information. The following results were obtained for the control group: volume = 34.3 cm3, count = 660316, and calculated liver radioactivity = 19251 /cm3.

Fig. 10b shows the experimental group, revealing the in vivo distribution of targeting AFSG-99mTc MNPs after in vitro liver magnetic targeting in New Zealand rabbits. The SPECT images obtained by whole-body CT show the accumulation of MNPs in the liver after magnetic targeted intervention for 30 min. The radioactivity uptake of the liver was also determined by region-of-interest technology, and the liver was investigated by CT and normalized mutual information (NMI). The following results were obtained for the experimental group: volume = 61.35 cm3, count = 4358070, and radioactivity uptake in the liver = 71036 /cm3. The data obtained from the control and experimental groups indicated that the uptake of radioactivity by the livers of the New Zealand rabbits subjected to magnetic targeted intervention was 3.69 times higher than that achieved in the livers of the rabbits without magnetic targeted intervention. The experimental results indicate that the YMC-11 medical pulsed magnetic field generator successfully facilitated the sequestration of the AFSG-99mTc MNPs that circulated in the bloodstream, achieving a clear targeted effect. Further, these findings indicate that AFSG-99mTc MNPs can pass through the blood circulation and accumulate at targeted areas under the action of magnetic targeting, providing the basis for in vivo targeted drug delivery experiments.

In the present study, Al-Fe3O4 MNPs had been prepared by solid–solid reaction between Fe3O4 MNPs and formic acid hydrazide. The Al3+ doped Fe3O4 MNPs contained Al3+ in a 2 %-10 % molar ratio. Mössbauer spectroscopy and saturation magnetization revealed that Al-Fe3O4 MNPs had the largest saturation magnetization value (82.3 emu/g) of the AFS MNPs, because small non-magnetic Al3+ ions prefer to occupy the tetrahedral sites in Fe3O4 MNPs. The core–shell AFS MNPs had been successfully prepared by SiO2 coating around Al-Fe3O4 MNPs with the aid of a bipolar surfactant. The particles were labeled with 99mTc by attaching APTES through the silanol group to the silica surface with subsequent chemical grafting of GQDs onto the APTES via amide bonding. The AFSG-99mTc MNPs were injected into the marginal ear veins of healthy male New Zealand rabbits, as visualized by SPECT imaging, and healthy female mice were injected with AFSG-99mTc MNPs via their tail veins to perform in vivo AFSG-99mTc MNPs targeting.

The in vivo distribution of AFSG-99mTc MNPs was visualized by SPECT imaging of healthy male New Zealand rabbits. The findings revealed high uptake of 99mTc by the liver: the particles of AFSG-99mTc MNPs with high saturation magnetization were able to travel through the blood circulation and accumulate at targeted areas under the action of magnetic targeting.

Weiwei Huan: Validation. Mengyang Dong: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jie Li: . Meng Xie: Methodology. Yan Huang: Visualization, Investigation. Riccardo Carlini: Writing – review & editing. Yuxiang Yang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Frank Kitching, MSc., from Liwen Bianji (Edanz) (www.liwenbianji.cn) for editing the English text of a draft of this manuscript. The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 20577010 and 20971043), and by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmmm.2023.171360.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
